title: वायु अष्टकम्
devanagariText:
॥ श्री वायु अष्टकम् ॥

मातरिश्वानं महाभागं प्राणापानविधायकम्।
पञ्चप्राणस्वरूपं त्वां वायुदेवं नमाम्यहम्॥ १॥

सर्वगं सर्वभूतस्थं निराकारं निरामयम्।
प्रत्यक्षं सर्वलोकानां वायुदेवं नमाम्यहम्॥ २॥

ध्वजं गृह्णन्तमायान्तं शब्दं वहन्तमन्तरम्।
गन्धप्रवाहकं दिव्यं वायुदेवं नमाम्यहम्॥ ३॥

वृक्षान् कम्पयतां श्रेष्ठं मेघान् वाहयतां वरम्।
समुद्रतरङ्गकर्तारं वायुदेवं नमाम्यहम्॥ ४॥

यज्ञे हवींषि वहन्तं देवेभ्यो हव्यवाहनम्।
ऋत्विजां सहकारं च वायुदेवं नमाम्यहम्॥ ५॥

सृष्टौ प्राणं प्रयच्छन्तं संहारे प्राणहारकम्।
सृष्टिस्थितिलयानां च वायुदेवं नमाम्यहम्॥ ६॥

हनूमज्जनकं वन्दे भीमस्य पितरं शुभम्।
बलवीर्यप्रदातारं वायुदेवं नमाम्यहम्॥ ७॥

नमस्ते वायवे तुभ्यं त्वमेव प्रत्यक्षं ब्रह्मासि।
त्वामेव प्रत्यक्षं ब्रह्म वदिष्यामि नमो वायवे॥ ८॥

॥ इति श्री वायु अष्टकम् सम्पूर्णम् ॥
transliteration:
|| Shri Vayu Ashtakam ||

Matarishvanam Mahabhagam Pranapana Vidhayakam |
Panchaprana Svarupam Tvam Vayudevam Namamy Aham || 1 ||

Sarvagam Sarvabhutastham Nirakaram Niramayam |
Pratyaksham Sarvalokanam Vayudevam Namamy Aham || 2 ||

Dhvajam Grihantam Ayantam Shabdam Vahantam Antaram |
Gandhapravahakam Divyam Vayudevam Namamy Aham || 3 ||

Vrikshan Kampayatam Shreshtham Meghan Vahayatam Varam |
Samudra Tarangakartaram Vayudevam Namamy Aham || 4 ||

Yajne Havimshi Vahantam Devebhyo Havyavahanam |
Ritvijam Sahakaram Cha Vayudevam Namamy Aham || 5 ||

Srishtau Pranam Prayachchhantam Samhare Pranaharakam |
Srishtisthitilayanam Cha Vayudevam Namamy Aham || 6 ||

Hanumajjanakam Vande Bhimasya Pitaram Shubham |
Balaveerya Pradataram Vayudevam Namamy Aham || 7 ||

Namaste Vayave Tubhyam Tvameva Pratyaksham Brahmasi |
Tvameva Pratyaksham Brahma Vadishyami Namo Vayave || 8 ||

|| Iti Shri Vayu Ashtakam Sampurnam ||
hindiMeaning:
मातरिश्वा, महाभाग, प्राण-अपान के विधायक, पञ्चप्राणस्वरूप वायुदेव को नमन।

सर्वव्यापी, सर्वभूतों में स्थित, निराकार, निरामय, सर्वलोकों में प्रत्यक्ष वायुदेव को नमन।

ध्वज को हिलाते, शब्द को वहन करते, गन्ध का प्रवाह करने वाले दिव्य वायुदेव को नमन।

वृक्षों को कम्पाने में श्रेष्ठ, मेघों को ले जाने वाले, समुद्र की तरंगें उठाने वाले वायुदेव को नमन।

यज्ञ में हवि को देवताओं तक पहुँचाने वाले, ऋत्विजों के सहकारी वायुदेव को नमन।

सृष्टि में प्राण देने वाले, संहार में प्राण लेने वाले वायुदेव को नमन।

हनुमान के जनक, भीम के पिता, बल-वीर्य प्रदान करने वाले वायुदेव को नमन।

हे वायु! तुम्हें नमस्कार — तुम्हीं प्रत्यक्ष ब्रह्म हो (तैत्तिरीय उपनिषद्)।
benefits: Eight sacred verses praising Vayu as manifest Brahman
Contains the Taittiriya Upanishad declaration 'Tvameva Pratyaksham Brahma'
Invokes vital energy, strength and freedom from respiratory ailments
Invokes Vayu as father of Hanuman and Bhima for strength
Ideal for pranayama practitioners and those seeking vitality
description: Among the most cherished prayers to Lord Vayu, Vayu Ashtakam (वायु अष्टकम्) stands as an eight-verse devotional hymn. This sacred composition is sourced from Vayu Purana / Taittiriya Upanishad and continues to inspire devotees worldwide. Devotees recite this prayer to grant vital energy, strength and freedom from respiratory ailments and invoke Vayu as father of Hanuman and Bhima for strength. It is also said to contain the Taittiriya Upanishad declaration 'Tvameva Pratyaksham Brahma'. This sacred text is ideally recited on Saturday (Shanivar) for enhanced blessings. Comprising 8 verses, it can be completed in approximately 4 minutes, making it suitable for daily devotional practice. On this page, you can read the complete Vayu Ashtakam in Devanagari Sanskrit with English transliteration, Hindi meaning (arth), and free PDF download for offline recitation.

seoFaqs.question: What is the Vayu Ashtakam?
seoFaqs.answer: The Vayu Ashtakam (वायु अष्टकम् — Eight Verses to Vayu) is a short 8-verse Sanskrit hymn praising Bhagavan Vayu, the Vedic deity of wind, breath and the cosmic life-force (prana), drawn from the Vayu Purana and the Taittiriya Upanishad tradition. Each verse ends in the refrain Vayu-devam namamy aham — I bow to Vayu Deva. The eight verses systematically describe Vayu in his cosmic functions: (1) Matarishvanam Mahabhagam — the great-fortuned heaven-dweller who shapes prana and apana, (2) Sarvagam Sarva-bhutastham Nirakaram Niramayam — all-pervading, abiding in all beings, formless, without disease, (3) Dhvajam grihantam — moving the flag, carrying sound and fragrance, (4) Vrikshan kampayatam — shaking trees, propelling clouds and ocean waves, (5) Yajne havimshi vahantam — carrying the offerings to the devas in yajna as Havyavahana, (6) Srishtau pranam prayachchhantam — giving prana at creation and removing it at dissolution, (7) Hanumaj-janakam — the father of Hanuman and Bhima, granting strength and valour, and (8) the climactic Tvam-eva pratyaksham Brahmasi — You alone are the manifest Brahman. (Source: Vayu Purana / Taittiriya Upanishad tradition.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the meaning of Tvam-eva Pratyaksham Brahmasi in the Vayu Ashtakam?
seoFaqs.answer: Verse 8 of the Vayu Ashtakam — Namaste Vayave tubhyam tvam-eva pratyaksham Brahmasi — directly cites one of the most famous mantras of the Taittiriya Upanishad (Shikshavalli 1.1.1): Namo Brahmane, namaste Vayo, tvam-eva pratyaksham Brahmasi, tvam-eva pratyaksham Brahma vadishyami — Salutations to Brahman; salutations to you, O Vayu! You alone are the manifest Brahman; you alone I shall declare to be the manifest Brahman. This Upanishadic declaration is profoundly significant — Vayu (wind / prana / breath) is identified as the most directly accessible (pratyaksha) form of the absolute Brahman in this physical world, since prana is the vital force present in every living being. The mantra is recited daily by orthodox Brahmins at the start of every Vedic study session as the Shanti Patha of the Taittiriya Upanishad. By including this mantra as its concluding verse, the Vayu Ashtakam transforms a simple devotional hymn into a complete Upanishadic meditation on Vayu as Brahman. (Sources: Taittiriya Upanishad 1.1; Vayu Ashtakam verse 8.)

seoFaqs.question: Why is Vayu called the father of Hanuman and Bhima?
seoFaqs.answer: Verse 7 of the Vayu Ashtakam praises Vayu as Hanumaj-janakam vande Bhimasya pitaram shubham — I salute the father of Hanuman, the auspicious father of Bhima. The Ramayana records that Hanuman was born to Anjana through the grace of Vayu Deva — when Anjana was performing tapas to obtain a son, Vayu carried the prasada-payasa from the putra-kameshti yajna of King Dasharatha (intended for his queens) and dropped it into Anjana's outstretched hands; she became pregnant by Vayu's grace and gave birth to Hanuman, who is therefore called Vayu-putra, Anjaneya and Maruti. Similarly, the Mahabharata records that Kunti, using the mantra given to her by sage Durvasa, invoked Vayu to obtain her second son Bhima — born of supernatural strength like Hanuman. The Madhva sampradaya identifies all three — Hanuman, Bhima and Madhvacharya — as the three avataras of Vayu in the Treta, Dvapara and Kali yugas respectively, making Vayu the cosmic father of all great strength-warriors. (Sources: Valmiki Ramayana; Mahabharata Adi Parva; Madhva sampradaya.)

seoFaqs.question: When should the Vayu Ashtakam be recited for maximum benefit?
seoFaqs.answer: The Vayu Ashtakam is most powerfully recited on Saturday (Shanivar — the day of Hanuman, son of Vayu, and of vata-dosha pacification), Tuesday (Mangalvar — the second day of Hanuman worship), Hanuman Jayanti (Chaitra Shukla Purnima), Bhima Ekadashi (Magha Shukla Ekadashi — Bhima's vrata day), during pranayama practice (since Vayu rules prana), at the start of yoga sessions, before any physical exertion or sports event (for strength), and as a Vedic shanti-mantra before any major Vedic study. The Madhva sampradaya devotees recite it daily as part of the Vayu-stuti collection. The phala-shruti tradition recommends recitation for those suffering from respiratory disorders (asthma, COPD, sinusitis), vata-related Ayurvedic imbalances (anxiety, joint pain, neurological disorders), and weakness or exhaustion — since Vayu is the cosmic giver of bala (strength) and prana (vital energy). (Sources: Madhva sampradaya; Hanuman upasana tradition.)

seoFaqs.question: What is Vayu's role in Hindu cosmology — five pranas, yajna and creation?
seoFaqs.answer: The Vayu Ashtakam encodes Vayu's complete cosmological role in just eight verses. (1) Pancha-prana svarupa (verse 1): Vayu manifests in the body as the five vital airs — prana (in-breath, head and chest), apana (out-breath, downward, eliminative), vyana (circulating, all-pervading), udana (rising, throat and voice), and samana (digestive, central). All bodily functions depend on these five pranas, which is why Vayu is called the breath of life. (2) Sarvagam (verse 2): Vayu is all-pervading — present everywhere as air, sound, smell and touch. (3) Havya-vahana (verse 5): Vayu carries the yajna offerings to the devas as the very element that makes Agni's flames rise. (4) Srishtau pranam prayachchhantam samhare prana-harakam (verse 6): Vayu gives prana at creation and removes it at death — making him the literal lord of life and death. The Vayu Purana, Brahmavaivarta Purana and Markandeya Purana all preserve this complete vayu-mahatmya theology. (Sources: Vayu Purana; Taittiriya Upanishad; Yoga Sutras of Patanjali.)

seoFaqs.question: What benefits does the Vayu Ashtakam grant according to tradition?
seoFaqs.answer: Tradition attributes the following blessings to sincere recitation of the Vayu Ashtakam: (1) Bala-virya-pradanam (verse 7) — bestowal of strength and valour, the same Hanuman-like physical and mental power that comes from Vayu's grace, (2) cure of respiratory disorders — asthma, breathlessness, sinusitis, lung weakness, (3) pacification of vata dosha — the Ayurvedic principle ruling Vayu in the body, related to anxiety, insomnia, joint pain, dryness and neurological disorders, (4) success in pranayama practice — since Vayu is the very deity of prana being controlled, (5) the merit of yajna — since Vayu carries every offering to the devas, (6) protection from sudden death (akala-mrityu), since Vayu rules the prana that sustains life, (7) Brahma-sakshatkara through Vayu as pratyaksha-Brahman as the Taittiriya Upanishad declares, (8) blessings for athletes, soldiers, swimmers, mountaineers and yoga practitioners — all professions requiring exceptional bala-virya, and (9) victory in the way Bhima conquered the Mahabharata war. (Sources: Vayu upasana tradition; Madhva sampradaya phala-shruti.)
